name: Yemoja
title: Yoruba - Yemoja - Mother of Waters and Fertility
epithets: Yeye Omo Eja (Mother Whose Children Are Fish)

ifaNarratives.title: Yemoja Gives Birth to the Orisha
ifaNarratives.content: In one origin story, Yemoja's body swelled until it could contain no more. When she burst, the waters flooded forth to create the rivers and oceans. From her womb came many of the major Orisha - Shango, Ogun, Oshun, Oya, and others (the specific children vary by tradition). Her body became the earth, and her breasts became two hills from which flow the source of the Ogun River. This myth establishes Yemoja as the primordial mother from whom life itself flows.

ifaNarratives.title: Yemoja and Orunmila - The Origin of Divination
ifaNarratives.content: Yemoja was once married to Orunmila, the Orisha of wisdom and divination. She was his first wife and helped him establish the Ifa system. However, Orunmila forbade her from touching his sacred divination implements. One day, curious about her husband's powers, Yemoja secretly used the ikin (palm nuts). When Orunmila discovered this, he banished her. Heartbroken, Yemoja went to live in the river, taking with her knowledge of divination. This is why women can become Iyanifa (female diviners) and why water is used in many divination rituals.

ifaNarratives.title: Yemoja's Protection of Women
ifaNarratives.content: A young woman was being pursued by an evil spirit who wished to steal her unborn child. She ran and ran until she reached the river's edge with nowhere to go. Crying out to Yemoja, she threw herself into the water. But instead of drowning, she felt the Great Mother's arms embrace her. Yemoja carried her downstream to safety and killed the evil spirit with her waves. When the woman gave birth, Yemoja blessed the child. This is why pregnant women pray to Yemoja for protection, and why births in Yoruba tradition are often accompanied by offerings to the water.

ifaNarratives.title: Yemoja and Olokun - The Depths and the Surface
ifaNarratives.content: Yemoja and Olokun (Orisha of the deep ocean) have a complex relationship. In some traditions, Olokun is Yemoja's mother or father; in others, they are aspects of the same force. Olokun rules the unfathomable depths - the mysteries beyond human comprehension. Yemoja rules the surface and the waters that touch the shore - the aspect of the sea that interacts with humanity. Together they represent the full power of the ocean, from sunlit waves to the abyss where no light reaches.

ifaNarratives.title: The Middle Passage - Yemoja's Grief
ifaNarratives.content: When her children were stolen from Africa and forced onto slave ships, Yemoja wept. Her tears filled the Atlantic Ocean with her sorrow. But she did not abandon her children - she followed them across the water, protecting those she could, receiving those who died at sea into her watery embrace. In the Americas, she is honored as the spirit who accompanied the enslaved across the ocean and who continues to protect her children in the diaspora. This is why she is so powerfully venerated in Cuba, Brazil, and throughout the Caribbean.

sacredSites.nigeria: In Nigeria, Yemoja's primary sacred site is the Ogun River in Ogun State, where an annual festival is held. The river's source at the hills is considered especially sacred.
sacredSites.diaspora: In the diaspora, the ocean itself is her shrine - devotees make offerings at the beach, especially at sunset or during the full moon.

festivals.nigeria: The Yemoja Festival in Abeokuta, Nigeria, is a major annual celebration featuring processions to the river, offerings of food and flowers, and prayers for fertility and protection.
festivals.brazil: In Brazil, Festa de Iemanja on February 2nd sees millions of people at beaches offering flowers, perfume, and mirrors to the sea. In Rio, boats carry offerings to be thrown into the ocean.

invocations: Fertility - Women who cannot conceive pray to Yemoja
Safe pregnancy and childbirth - She protects mothers and newborns
Protection of children - All children are under her care
Emotional healing - She soothes grief and depression
Dreams and intuition - She rules the unconscious depths
Protection at sea - Sailors and fishermen honor her
Abundance - As mother of fish, she provides sustenance
protection.warning: But Yemoja is not only gentle. Like the sea, she can be terrible in her wrath. Those who harm children or mothers may feel her fury in drownings, floods, and storms. The ocean that nurtures can also destroy.

cuba.name: Yemaya
cuba.notes: Syncretized with a Black Madonna whose church is at Havana's harbor. She has many 'caminos' (paths) - Yemaya Okute (warrior aspect), Yemaya Olokun (deep ocean), Yemaya Asesu (messenger). Colors: blue and white. Initiation involves elaborate ceremonies near water.

brazil.name: Iemanja
syncretism: Our Lady of the Immaculate Conception
brazil.notes: Queen of the sea, one of the most popular Orixa. February 2nd (Festa de Iemanja) is a national celebration. Offerings of flowers, mirrors, and perfume are sent to sea on small boats. The mermaid image is strongly associated with her.

haiti.name: La Siren (The Mermaid)
haiti.notes: She represents the sea itself. Ceremonies include offerings to the ocean. She may possess devotees during rituals, causing them to move as if swimming. Her songs are among the most beautiful in the tradition. Also associated with Agwe's wife.

trinidad.name: Yemanja/Emanja
trinidad.notes: Associated with the sea and motherhood. Ceremonies for her are held at beaches. She is called upon for fertility, protection of children, and safe sea travel. Her colors are blue and white.

description: The universal mother who gave birth to many of the Orisha, Yemoja is the great nurturer and protector. Her realm is the ocean and the Ogun River in Nigeria. She embodies the deep, mysterious, life-giving power of the waters. Yemoja fiercely protects women, especially pregnant women and mothers, and all who dwell near the sea. Her embrace is like the ocean - vast, nurturing, and capable of tremendous power when her children are threatened.
summary: Great Mother Orisha of the ocean and rivers who gave birth to many Orisha and fiercely protects women, children, and all who honor her.

consorts.name: Obatala
consorts.nature: Considered her husband in many traditions. Their union represents the union of water and land, creation and form.

consorts.name: Orunmila
consorts.nature: Her husband in some stories. Their separation explains aspects of divination tradition.

consorts.name: Olokun
consorts.nature: In some lineages, not a spouse but closely connected - representing different aspects of the sea.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of Yemoja
extendedContent.content:
Yemoja's origins are deeply rooted in the creation narratives of Yoruba mythology. She is often described as the daughter of Olokun, the primordial deity of the ocean, further solidifying her connection to the watery realm. Accounts vary, but a common thread depicts her as emanating directly from the vast, formless waters, embodying the very essence of the ocean's life-giving properties. Some narratives suggest that Yemoja predates many other Orisha, existing as a fundamental force even before the established pantheon. 

Her name, a contraction of 'Yeye mo eja', translates to 'Mother of Fish', hinting at her maternal role and abundance. This etymology is significant because it directly links her to fertility and the sustenance of life within the aquatic environment. Furthermore, some traditions posit that Yemoja was initially a river goddess, specifically associated with the Ogun River in Nigeria, before her dominion expanded to encompass the oceans. This transition reflects the Yoruba understanding of interconnectedness between rivers and oceans as vital components of the Earth's ecosystem.

The union of Yemoja with different Orisha also contributes to the complex tapestry of her origin. One prominent myth recounts her marriage to Orisha Oko, a deity associated with agriculture and farming. From this union, many powerful Orisha are said to have been born, including Ogun, Shango, and Oya, highlighting Yemoja's central role as a universal mother figure within the Yoruba pantheon. Her children represent diverse aspects of nature and human experience, underscoring her profound influence on the cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples Dedicated to Yemoja
extendedContent.content:
Worship of Yemoja is widespread throughout the Yoruba diaspora, particularly in Nigeria, Brazil (where she is known as Iemanjá), Cuba, and other regions with significant Afro-Caribbean populations. Rituals dedicated to Yemoja often involve offerings of fruits, flowers (especially white roses), perfumes, and other items associated with femininity and the ocean. Blue and white colors, symbolizing the sea and purity, are frequently used in her shrines and during ceremonies.

In Nigeria, the Ogun River is considered sacred to Yemoja, and annual festivals are held along its banks to honor her. These festivals typically involve drumming, dancing, singing, and elaborate processions. Devotees offer prayers and sacrifices, seeking her blessings for fertility, protection, and general well-being. Similar celebrations occur in coastal communities throughout the diaspora, often coinciding with significant dates like New Year's Eve in Brazil, where elaborate offerings are set afloat on the ocean.

Temples dedicated to Yemoja are found in various forms, ranging from simple altars in homes to elaborate structures with dedicated priests and priestesses. These spaces serve as focal points for communication with the deity, providing a place for devotees to express their devotion and seek guidance. Priests and priestesses play a crucial role in interpreting Yemoja's messages and conducting rituals on behalf of the community. The specific practices and traditions associated with Yemoja worship can vary from one region to another, reflecting the diverse cultural adaptations and interpretations of her veneration.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation of Yemoja
extendedContent.content:
Yemoja's iconography is rich with symbolism, reflecting her multifaceted nature as a mother, protector, and ruler of the oceans. She is often depicted as a beautiful and powerful woman, adorned in flowing blue and white garments, symbolizing the colors of the sea. Her attire frequently includes cowrie shells, which represent wealth, fertility, and the connection to the ocean's depths. Crowns or headpieces adorned with fish, waves, or other aquatic motifs further emphasize her dominion over the watery realm.

In visual representations, Yemoja is often portrayed surrounded by fish, shells, and other marine life, reinforcing her association with the ocean's abundance and the life it sustains. Her posture is often regal and maternal, reflecting her role as a nurturing and protective figure. While artistic interpretations may vary across different cultures and regions, the core elements of her iconography remain consistent, emphasizing her connection to water, femininity, and motherhood.

Beyond visual representations, Yemoja is also symbolized by specific numbers and colors within the Yoruba belief system. The number seven is frequently associated with her, representing completeness and spiritual perfection. Her colors, blue and white, evoke the vastness and purity of the ocean. These symbolic associations are incorporated into various aspects of her worship, from the colors of her altars to the number of offerings presented to her.

extendedContent.title: Yemoja's Role in the Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Yemoja plays a crucial role in maintaining the cosmic balance within the Yoruba worldview. As the mother of many Orisha, she represents the life-giving force that sustains the universe. Her dominion over the oceans and rivers connects her to the fundamental elements of creation and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. The ebb and flow of the tides, the abundance of marine life, and the power of the ocean storms are all seen as manifestations of her divine energy.

Her connection to fertility and motherhood extends beyond the physical realm, influencing the spiritual and emotional well-being of individuals and communities. She is invoked for assistance with childbirth, protection during pregnancy, and guidance in raising children. Her nurturing qualities are also sought by those seeking emotional healing, comfort, and a sense of belonging. Yemoja's influence permeates various aspects of human life, from personal relationships to communal harmony.

Furthermore, Yemoja's role as a protector of women and marginalized communities underscores her commitment to social justice and equality. She is often invoked to defend those who are vulnerable and oppressed, and her strength and resilience serve as an inspiration for those facing adversity. Her presence in the Yoruba pantheon highlights the importance of female power and the recognition of women's contributions to society. Yemoja's cosmic role is not merely one of creation and sustenance, but also one of justice, compassion, and unwavering support for those in need.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Goddess of Magic & Motherhood | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings to Yemoja often include fruits, flowers (especially white roses), perfumes, and soaps. These offerings are typically placed near bodies of water, such as the ocean or rivers, to honor her connection to these realms.

worshipPractices.name: Dance and Song
worshipPractices.description: Devotees express their devotion through dance and song, often invoking Yemoja's name and attributes. These performances are performed with reverence and joy, celebrating her power and benevolence.

festivals.name: Odun Yemoja
festivals.description: During Odun Yemoja, devotees gather at the Ogun River or the ocean to offer prayers and sacrifices. There are processions, drumming, dancing, and feasting, all dedicated to honoring Yemoja and seeking her blessings for fertility, protection, and prosperity.

cultural.modernLegacy: Yemoja's influence extends beyond traditional Yoruba religion, inspiring artists, writers, and musicians worldwide. She appears in literature, music, and visual arts as a symbol of motherhood, femininity, and the power of nature, often depicted as a beautiful and powerful woman emerging from the ocean. Her image and story continue to resonate with those seeking connection to the divine feminine and the life-giving force of water.

associations.name: Ocean
associations.significance: Yemoja is the queen of the ocean, embodying its vastness, depth, and life-giving properties.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Blue represents the ocean's color and Yemoja's connection to the waters and serenity.

associations.name: Motherhood
associations.significance: Yemoja is the universal mother, symbolizing nurturing, protection, and unconditional love.
Seven is associated with Yemoja in some traditions, representing completeness and spiritual power.

associations.name: Fish
associations.significance: Fish represent fertility, abundance, and Yemoja's dominion over the aquatic realm.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon's connection to tides and feminine energy links it to Yemoja's watery domain and maternal aspects.

associations.name: Water Lily
associations.significance: Water lilies symbolize purity, beauty, and Yemoja's grace, flourishing in her watery domain.

companions.name: Ogun
companions.description: Ogun is Yemoja's husband in some traditions. Their relationship is complex and represents the interplay of water and iron/technology.

companions.name: Olokun
companions.description: Olokun is another Orisha of the ocean, often associated with the depths and mysteries Yemoja doesn't directly control. They represent different aspects of the ocean's power.

companions.name: Oshun
companions.description: Oshun and Yemoja are often seen as sisters, representing different aspects of womanhood and water. They support and complement each other.

companions.name: Fish
companions.description: Fish are sacred to Yemoja as they reside in her domain. They serve as her messengers and symbols of her abundance.

companions.name: Egungun
companions.description: Yemoja protects and watches over the Egungun society. She provides strength and guidance to them.
